text: Some time later, this happened: Absalom son of David had a beautiful sister named Tamar, and David's son Amnon fell in love with her.
Amnon became so obsessed that he made himself sick over his sister Tamar, since she was a virgin and it seemed impossible for him to do anything about it.
But Amnon had a friend named Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah, and Jonadab was a very shrewd man.
Jonadab asked him, "Why do you look so worn down morning after morning, prince? Won't you tell me?" Amnon said, "I'm in love with Tamar, my brother Absalom's sister."
Jonadab told him, "Go to bed and act sick. When your father comes to see you, ask him to send your sister Tamar to feed you in your sight, so you can watch her prepare it and eat from her own hand."
So Amnon lay down and pretended to be ill. When the king came to see him, Amnon said, "Please let my sister Tamar come and make a couple of cakes in front of me, so I can eat from her hand."
David sent word to Tamar at the palace: "Go to your brother Amnon's house and prepare food for him."
So Tamar went to her brother Amnon's house, where he was lying down; she took dough, kneaded it, shaped cakes in front of him, and baked them.
Then she took the pan and served them to him, but he refused to eat and told everyone to leave the room; so they all went out.
Amnon said to Tamar, "Bring the food into the bedroom so I can eat from your hand." Tamar took the cakes she had made and carried them in to her brother Amnon.
But as she brought them close for him to eat, he grabbed her and said, "Come, lie with me, my sister."
She answered him, "No, my brother! Do not force me — such a thing is not done in Israel. Do not commit this disgraceful act."
"Where could I ever carry my shame? And you would become like one of the shameful fools of Israel. Please, speak to the king — he will not refuse to let you have me."
But he refused to listen to her, and being stronger than she was, he forced her and lay with her.
Then Amnon was overcome with hatred for her — a hatred greater than the love he had felt before — and he said to her, "Get up! Get out!"
She said to him, "No, sending me away now is a worse wrong than what you've already done to me!" But he still would not listen.
He called the young servant who attended him and said, "Throw this woman out of here and bolt the door behind her."
Now she was wearing a long robe with sleeves, the kind worn by the king's virgin daughters. So his servant put her out and bolted the door after her.
Tamar put ashes on her head, tore the ornamented robe she was wearing, put her hand on her head, and went away weeping loudly as she went.

Her brother Absalom asked her, "Has your brother Amnon been with you? Be quiet about it for now, my sister — he's your brother; don't take this to heart." So Tamar lived on in her brother Absalom's house, a desolate woman.
When King David heard about all of this, he was furious.
But Absalom said nothing to Amnon, good or bad, because he hated Amnon for violating his sister Tamar.
Two full years later, Absalom had sheepshearers working at Baal-hazor, near Ephraim, and he invited all of the king's sons.
Absalom went to the king and said, "Your servant has sheepshearers now — would the king and his officials please come along with your servant?"
The king replied, "No, my son, we shouldn't all go — it would be too much of a burden on you." Absalom pressed him, but he still would not go, though he blessed him instead.
Then Absalom said, "If not, then please let my brother Amnon come with us." The king asked, "Why should he go with you?"
But Absalom kept pressing until the king let Amnon and all his other sons go with him.

Absalom instructed his young men, "Watch closely — when Amnon's heart is merry with wine and I say, 'Strike Amnon,' kill him then. Don't be afraid; I'm the one giving the order. Be strong and brave about it."
So Absalom's men did to Amnon exactly as Absalom had commanded. Then all the king's other sons got up, each mounted his mule, and fled.
While they were still on the road, word reached David that Absalom had struck down all the king's sons and not a single one of them was left.
The king got up, tore his clothes, and lay down on the ground, and all his attendants standing nearby tore their clothes too.

But Jonadab, son of David's brother Shimeah, said, "My lord shouldn't think they've killed all the young men, the king's sons — only Amnon is dead. Absalom has had this planned ever since the day Amnon violated his sister Tamar."
"So my lord the king shouldn't take it to heart as though all his sons were dead — only Amnon is dead."
Meanwhile Absalom fled. The watchman on duty looked up and saw a large crowd coming down the road behind him, from the side of the hill.
Jonadab said to the king, "Look, the king's sons are coming — it's exactly as your servant said."
As soon as he finished speaking, the king's sons arrived, weeping loudly, and the king and all his attendants wept bitterly as well.
Absalom fled and went to Talmai son of Ammihud, king of Geshur. David mourned for his son every day.
So Absalom stayed as a fugitive in Geshur for three years.
And King David's heart came to long for Absalom, since he had been comforted about Amnon's death.
